e728692a.title: meeting3

summaries.Lily: [Analyst] Open bugs exceed SLOs; next data metrics work stream to deliver updates addressing this. Provide update on previous conversations (point seven). FY22: maintain productivity.

e728692a.transcript:
Lily: Hi, this is Eric Johnson. It's February 18, 2021. And this is the engineering key review at GitLab.
Lily: So I've got number four in the agenda, which is a proposal to break up this meeting into four department key reviews. So currently, this is engineering.
Lily: Development quality, security, and UX infrastructure and support to their own key reviews already. I have the reasons why increased visibility able to go deeper.
Lily: Increase the objectivity with which my reports can manage their groups, allow me more time to focus on new markets and allow me to shift into more of a question asker mode, then generating content and answering questions in these meetings.
Lily: And but to avoid adding three net new meetings to stakeholders counters and proposed we do a sort of two month rotation. So month one development quality, go month two security and UX would go.
Lily: How do people feel about that proposal?
Lily: I think in the group conversations it's working really well. So I'm supportive and
Lily: this is the smallest thing. Maybe we need four meetings a month, like it's the biggest department, it's super central. But you proposed this. I don't, I could see either way. So let's stick with the proposal.
Lily: Cool, we'll try it and we'll, we can be flexible. I mean, development is larger. Maybe they get more frequently or something at that, but we'll see how it goes.
Lily: Alright, and then I've got number five, which is we've got R&D overall MR rate. And we also have R&D wider MR rate, both as top level KPIs for engineering. So the difference between them in the simplest sense is that R&D wider MR rate includes both community contributions and community MRs.
Lily: The problems I see with this are that one, the wider MR rate, the one that includes internally and external MRs, it duplicates the overall MR rate, which is.
Lily: The wider MR rate should just be external, right. And an overall should be narrow plus wider.
Lily: Oh, yeah, like we say the wider community.
Lily: Right, right, right. Okay. So there's.
Lily: I have to check the taxonomy.
Lily: Well, like, can you, can you confirm that's that's that's since reasoning is my understanding as well.
Lily: Yeah, I believe water MR rate just captures community contributions.
Lily: Only and no internal.
Lily: Yeah, and the reason we measure data is that like one of the most likely failure modes is that we lose the community.
Lily: Yeah.
Lily: So we're just, or we're gets goofy is the, when you look at a specific team within the company, there could be contributions outside of that that aren't community contributions, they would be viewed community contributions by that group, but effectively, they're not from outside the company.
Lily: So that's why we use wider to kind of reflect that and narrow is very specific to the team.
Lily: The, are you saying that if someone in plan contributes to verify its viewed as wider?
Lily: Not quite that plan, plan and verify are just fine.
Lily: It's when you look at like the development versus infrastructure infrastructure will oftentimes contribute to developments work, but it won't be counted as MRs.
Lily: Okay, that's a, that's a potential bit of funkiness that we should talk about separately. I didn't have that in my sort of critique of this, but that, that doesn't necessarily make intuitive sense to me.
Lily: So, so then I think part of my critique of this can be thrown out because it's not as duplicative as I thought, but I still think there's a problem with R&D wider MR rate, which is this thing doesn't really move.
Lily: And part because it's a, it's a rate so it feels like the way to drive this up is to specifically drive community authors to contribute more than one MR per month.
Lily: That's how this moves up because it's a, it's a productivity rate like we use internally.
Lily: And that doesn't necessarily feel like the right thing because there's scenarios in which this goes up, we've actually got less contributions overall and less contributors overall.
Lily: Wait, wait, wait a second. So you, you're saying that R&D wider MR rate is number MRs per external contributor.
Lily: Oh my goodness, that should not be the thing. It should be contributions for GitLab team member. So the country, those, the, the thing above the division is the external ones.
Lily: The thing below is the number of team members at GitLab.
Lily: Is that the case, Lily?
Lily: I'm checking right now.
Lily: I think so in our, MRs per team member. So unless we start calling people outside the company team member, then it shouldn't be done.
Lily: Yeah, just clarifying here. So our numerator is community contributions and then our denominator is GitLab team members.
Lily: So we, so what we're doing there, Eric, is we're not trying to say how many, how many MRs does someone send if they send something or saying how many MRs from external do we get for the size of our organization?
Lily: Sorry, I have a childhood emergency outside the door.
Lily: So the, maybe explain the context behind this. The context is, as we grow as a company, we should make sure we, we keep the community up. Like the logical thing is for the community to flatline and the size of the org to go on before you start.
Lily: You've kind of outgrown the wider community.
Lily: Yeah, and what I'm seeing is we, we created this pre sophisticated taxonomy with prefixes and postfixes to talk about these things.
Lily: But in reality, we've only got two of them and it's, we keep forgetting, we have a hard time discussing this thing. So I'd rather just name them simply two names for what they are rather than, than using the taxonomy.
Lily: But also like in F, I have this proposal of like, what if we just track as a KPI, the percentage of total MRs that come from the community over time, and we would see that drop.
Lily: I love that. I love that. Let's do that instead.
Lily: Okay.
Lily: But the thing, the thing why we have this complex thing is because you can game that you want to game that you just produce fewer MRs with the engineers that get lot.
Lily: So if you drive that really hard and say this is your number one goal, it's very easy to achieve is just tell all your engineers to produce half.
Lily: Yeah, so we have, we have different metrics to prevent that from happening the same way that like support SLA is an asset kind of buttress one another.
Lily: I think we're, we're robust to that, but simplifying this would make these conversations.
Lily: If you as our CTO don't even understand them, we went overboard. So I'm well, I support if I understand them. And I forgot and I was agreeing this stuff this morning, I'm like, there's a problem with this and then you just remind me of the context.
Lily: So yeah, I can't really my head.
Lily: But percentage that come from the community. I love that. It's what all our investors ask about. Let's do that.
Lily: Okay, cool. So Lily, if you can work with Max to make that transition, I'll be great. And I'll hold the one that we're talking about. That's from your mastery. Thanks.
Lily: I'm on the call. Sorry, it was a bit late.
Lily: Timeline, we do have PIs on the raw number of community MRs and we can make the shift. And why they're confirming from the definition, I think why they only counts for community. And that's, that's what the definition is.
Lily: Cool.
Lily: Alright, so number six, then Christopher.
Lily: Sorry, I was looking up to see if I had the percentage graph, because I think we played around with this at one point and had a draft of that.
Lily: Probably about five months back if I can remember Lily.
Lily: Just in FYI month of February, if you were looking at any particular metrics, particularly in development at MR rate.
Lily: We haven't had updates in four days. There's apparently a lag issue that's been problematic for the data team to basically get
Lily: updated metrics and they're working on that.
Lily: I'm sorry, Mack, you get the next one.
Lily: So yeah, there's some some color there on on the on medication, the lag later on.
Lily: On to on to seven.
Lily: I said FYI in addition to the TVI status.
Lily: I'm sorry, Mack, I wanted to just touch on the postgres replication issue there real quick. I've been trying to get my arms wrapped around it.
Lily: Do we have the right attention to this? This is kind of Eric. I don't know if you were commenting on
Lily: hinting towards this in the last meeting around some of the infrastructure improvements on the product side. I'm just not quite sure whose responsibility it is to focus on getting
Lily: a handle on some of the constraints we have on replication.
Lily: Yeah, the what I was mentioning in the in the product key review about an hour ago is I think is sort of like unrelated.
Lily: And so I think the DRI needs to be your kind of data engineering team, but of course there's a dependency on infrastructure because that's where the data is being piped from they do on that data source.
Lily: Yeah, I'll say for the replication lag on that slave host where I'm sorry, not the on the the
Lily: secondary host where
Lily: the data is being pulled from like infrastructure would be the DRI for that. And so any escalations, but I'll all own those. And I know we have an action plan for that as far as creating another dedicated host just for the data team to pull from.
Lily: Okay, I did ask I saw that issue and I did talk to Craig Gomes a little bit as well on the database side just to see if there's some database improvements and I'm still trying to figure out, you know, if it's truly just a dedicated computational sort of resource a server or if there's actually some some database tuning that needs to occur. Do you have a sense of that?
Lily: There's so I'd say it's it's three different things. It's having a dedicated host that doesn't have conflicting query traffic coming from other other workloads.
Lily: There are some tuning performance or tuning improvements to be made. And then there's also improvements in and this is where it does maybe relate a little bit to what the topic was in the last review basically be the overall demand on the database layer from from.com activities and like improving those. So it's it's definitely not just one of those things.
Lily: But one of the most specific actions we're going to take though is separating out and having a dedicated host so that we're just dealing with the profile of the data engineering traffic on there and not having conflicting query
Lily: conflicting queries affect the ability to update the replication.
Lily: Steve, I definitely want to partner with you on this one because I think the demand on those databases is only going to increase. It's not going down.
Lily: And I think we need to get. I'm still unclear on where to focus and to get the biggest bang for the buck. I think of the computational resource dedication. That's going to be a good thing, but I'll probably going to squeeze the balloon.
Lily: And then the next area will will honor itself.
Lily: Okay. I'll tell you what I'll put into the input key review for next week.
Lily: Okay.
Lily: And update on this issue.
Lily: Thank you, Steve.
Lily: So then back to mecon 7 or.
Lily: Yes.
Lily: Thank you. And Rob was in these assassins, then this morning as well.
Lily: Brian, we have the attention there.
Lily: Number seven, just provide provide an update on previous conversations.
Lily: We continue to improve defect tracking and against SLOs.
Lily: There is a first iteration, PI that we are experimenting to show percentage of defects meeting the SLOs key findings as ones are hovering at 80% as to at 60.
Lily: We've been focused mostly on S1 as to this point as hence why S3 and S4s are a lower.
Lily: And this will likely be the case.
Lily: We are also in point B are working on the measurement for average open bugs age.
Lily: This would give us a whole picture of what's what's left.
Lily: If the age goes up or down to be you're cleaning the backlog the average age should go down as well.
Lily: There's no PI yet, but I just want update and should have beyond this.
Lily: It's not a off track.
Lily: Number C Craig on S2.
Lily: Yeah, I just was looking through the charts and I noted that there was a spike in meantime to close and just wanted to see if you had any insight into that press.
Lily: This is the S2.
Lily: That's the S1 looked fine.
Lily: Yeah, this is where the point B on age and supplemental charts in the backend helps.
Lily: So I haven't seen a dip in age nor the count overall.
Lily: I think it's the latter.
Lily: We need to dig in a bit deeper in that and also that the data lag.
Lily: I would like to reevaluate when we have a whole picture when everything is synced in as well.
Lily: Christie, you have some insights.
Lily: Yeah, I'm just wondering if part of this could be the fact that we changed the severity across the board for MRs to S2.
Lily: And so we may have some older bugs in there that hadn't been addressed because they were at a lower severity.
Lily: Now we've moved them to S2 and maybe that caused a little spike.
Lily: That could be the case that we did in a limited fashion.
Lily: It won't be a huge volume.
Lily: We also iterated after that to pin on priority since product owns prioritization.
Lily: So I wouldn't account it entirely to that.
Lily: I mean, this isn't the in for a key review, but I know that they've gotten backed up on those issues.
Lily: So if some some good portion of those are in for created or related, then that may be lifting it as well.
Lily: I can take the deeper dig in and then provide an update next time.
Lily: I think we need extra debug slicing the data here.
Lily: So do you like to go to point eight?
Lily: Yeah, we are now measuring S1, S2, SLO achievement with closed bugs.
Lily: But if you then look at the number of bugs, it's exponential growth.
Lily: And then it will be trivial to us to achieve 100% SLO achievement.
Lily: If you just look at closed bugs, even though there would be a major problem in the company,
Lily: 99% of all bugs are overdue.
Lily: As long as I only close ones that are still within the SLO, I'll have great achievement.
Lily: So I think we shouldn't be looking at the closed bugs.
Lily: I think we should be looking at open bugs in the entire population.
Lily: More percentage of doses within the SLO time.
Lily: I think we do it in the wrong way.
Lily: Thanks for the thanks for the feedbacks that.
Lily: Hence why we wanted to have the average age to measure what's outside in the open.

Lily: So the key is go ahead.
Lily: So average age would get closer to it.
Lily: It's not what I'm proposing.
Lily: The closing is off the open bugs are percentages outside of SLO.
Lily: So display it as a percentage.
Lily: You do now just do it about the open bugs, not the close ones.
Lily: Got it.
Lily: Okay.
Lily: The exceeding SLO for open bugs.
Lily: Yeah, or open bugs that are within SLO.
Lily: So you have a chart that should go up and to the right like everything else.
Lily: Sounds great.
Lily: So the next data metrics work stream to deliver this.
Lily: Cool.
Lily: Thanks.
Lily: Thank you.
Lily: Thank you.
Lily: You'll have to figure out how to.
Lily: Because we like to be able to have charts that we can historically reconstruct if we need to.
Lily: So when tickets close out, you need to go through their history to figure out at best time when it was open.

Lily: That's a good point.

Lily: The quarter previous was point six or six tenths of a point and the quarter before that was a full point.
Lily: So we see this as an improvement, even though it was still a decline, but it's still a decline.

Lily: look back historically here, the goal here was actually lower and Christopher was ambitious and we
Lily: get raising and we kept meeting that. So it should stare step here and we could go back and reconstruct
Lily: that if we really wanted to. And then ignoring the sort of the seasonal dip here, we raised it to,
Lily: I think, 11 and then we realized we're kind of hitting that point of doing managing returns and the

Lily: but start to raise other things related to quality, security, availability, and whatnot. So that's
Lily: kind of what you are, you're seeing this bump as we raised it and we realized, okay, we shouldn't
Lily: raise it anymore and we brought it back down to 10. So 10 will be the dark going forward. I'm going
Lily: to try to get better a lot of other things while preventing this from dipping.
Lily: Cool. And I just want to call out that it's not necessarily like a higher MR rate should also
Lily: help to address security and quality and other things because you're more productive so you can
Lily: fix more things. So it's not necessarily opposite, but I agree with, let's hold the line 10 is
Lily: then is a great number and focus on other indicators to improve that makes it on a sense.
Lily: Cool. Well said.

Lily: Great. Well, thanks, everybody. And I'm going to go check on my four-year-old and see if she got

6f85c082.title: meeting2

summaries.Lily: [Analyst] Product owns prioritization; current defect tracking meets SLOs but open bugs exceed SLO. Action: Deliver data metrics work stream to address open bugs exceeding SLO.
